panel.origin: A word that names a person, place, thing, or idea — the 'who' or 'what' of a sentence.
panel.chemistry: Nouns can be common (dog) or proper (Rex), concrete (table) or abstract (freedom), singular or plural. They can be subjects, objects, or complements.
panel.mixology: Don't capitalize common nouns; only proper nouns (specific names) get a capital letter.
panel.examples: The teacher gave the students hope. (teacher, students, hope are nouns)

panel.origin: A word that stands in for a noun so you don't have to repeat it — he, she, it, they, this, who.
panel.chemistry: Pronouns replace a noun (the antecedent) and must match it in number and gender. Types include personal, possessive, relative, and demonstrative.
panel.mixology: Make sure it's always clear which noun a pronoun refers to — vague pronouns confuse readers.
panel.examples: Maria lost her keys, so she called a locksmith. (her, she replace Maria)

panel.origin: A word that expresses an action or a state of being — the engine of every sentence.
panel.chemistry: Every complete sentence needs a verb. Action verbs (run, think) show what the subject does; linking verbs (is, seem) connect it to a description.
panel.mixology: Don't confuse action verbs with linking verbs — 'She is happy' uses a linking verb, not an action.
panel.examples: Birds fly. The soup tastes salty. (fly = action, tastes = linking)

panel.origin: A word that describes or modifies a noun — telling which, what kind, or how many.
panel.chemistry: Adjectives answer 'what kind?', 'which one?', or 'how many?' They usually sit before the noun or after a linking verb.
panel.mixology: Use commas between adjectives only if you could swap their order or insert 'and' (a tall, dark stranger).
panel.examples: The red car. Seven loud dogs. The sky is blue. (red, seven, loud, blue)

panel.origin: A word that modifies a verb, adjective, or another adverb — telling how, when, where, or to what degree.
panel.chemistry: Many adverbs end in -ly (quickly), but not all (very, often, here). They add detail about manner, time, place, or intensity.
panel.mixology: Avoid piling on adverbs; a strong verb usually beats a weak verb plus an adverb ('sprinted' over 'ran quickly').
panel.examples: She sang beautifully. He arrived late. It's very cold. (beautifully, late, very)

panel.origin: A word that shows the relationship between a noun and other words — usually position, direction, or time.
panel.chemistry: Prepositions (in, on, at, under, before, between) introduce a phrase that links a noun to the rest of the sentence.
panel.mixology: The old 'never end a sentence with a preposition' rule is a myth — natural English often does ('What are you waiting for?').
panel.examples: The cat sat on the mat under the table. (on, under)

panel.origin: A word that joins words, phrases, or clauses together — and, but, or, because, although.
panel.chemistry: Coordinating conjunctions (FANBOYS: for, and, nor, but, or, yet, so) join equals; subordinating ones (because, while) attach a dependent clause.
panel.mixology: Use a comma before a coordinating conjunction only when it joins two complete sentences.
panel.examples: Tea or coffee? I stayed because it rained. (or, because)

panel.origin: A short word or phrase that expresses sudden emotion — Wow! Ouch! Oh no!
panel.chemistry: Interjections stand apart from the grammar of a sentence and are usually followed by an exclamation point or comma.
panel.mixology: Use them sparingly in formal writing; they're best for dialogue and casual tone.
panel.examples: Wow, that's amazing! Ouch! Well, I suppose so. (Wow, Ouch, Well)

panel.origin: The small words a, an, and the that introduce a noun and signal whether it's specific or general.
panel.chemistry: 'The' is definite (a specific thing); 'a/an' is indefinite (any one). Use 'an' before a vowel sound, 'a' before a consonant sound.
panel.mixology: It's 'an hour' (silent h, vowel sound) but 'a university' (sounds like 'yoo') — go by sound, not spelling.
panel.examples: The dog chased a cat for an hour. (the, a, an)

panel.origin: The verb form for actions happening now or that happen habitually.
panel.chemistry: Simple present states facts and habits (I walk); it also covers general truths. Third-person singular usually adds -s (he walks).
panel.mixology: Don't forget the -s on he/she/it ('she runs', not 'she run') — a very common error.
panel.examples: I work downtown. The sun rises in the east. She walks to school.

panel.origin: The verb form for actions that already happened and are now complete.
panel.chemistry: Regular verbs add -ed (walked); irregular verbs change form unpredictably (go→went, see→saw). It places the action firmly in the past.
panel.mixology: Irregular past forms must be memorized — 'I seen it' is wrong; it's 'I saw it' or 'I have seen it'.
panel.examples: She walked home. They went to the beach. I saw the movie.

panel.origin: The verb form for actions that haven't happened yet.
panel.chemistry: English forms the future with 'will' + verb, or with 'going to' for plans and intentions. There's no single future ending.
panel.mixology: 'Will' and 'going to' differ subtly: 'going to' suits planned intentions, 'will' suits spontaneous decisions and predictions.
panel.examples: I will call you. We are going to travel next year.

panel.origin: An aspect that links a past action to another time — using 'have/has/had' plus a past participle.
panel.chemistry: Present perfect (have done) connects past to now; past perfect (had done) shows one past action before another. It's about completion relative to a reference point.
panel.mixology: Use present perfect for unfinished time ('I have lived here for years'), simple past for finished time ('I lived there in 2010').
panel.examples: I have finished. She had left before we arrived.

panel.origin: An aspect showing an action in progress — using 'be' plus the -ing form.
panel.chemistry: The progressive (am/is/are/was + verb-ing) emphasizes that an action is ongoing rather than complete.
panel.mixology: Some 'state' verbs resist the progressive — say 'I know', not 'I am knowing'.
panel.examples: I am reading. They were sleeping. She will be waiting.

panel.origin: Two ways to arrange a sentence: active (subject acts) or passive (subject is acted upon).
panel.chemistry: Active: 'The dog bit the man.' Passive: 'The man was bitten by the dog.' Passive uses 'be' + past participle and can drop the doer.
panel.mixology: Prefer active voice for clarity and energy; use passive deliberately when the doer is unknown or unimportant.
panel.examples: The committee made the decision. (active) / The decision was made. (passive)

panel.origin: The form of a verb that signals whether something is a fact, a command, or a hypothetical.
panel.chemistry: Indicative states facts (You are here); imperative gives commands (Be here); subjunctive expresses wishes or unreal conditions (If I were you).
panel.mixology: The subjunctive 'were' survives in set phrases — 'If I were you', not 'If I was you', in formal usage.
panel.examples: She is ready. (indicative) Be ready! (imperative) I wish I were ready. (subjunctive)

panel.origin: The two essential halves of a sentence: the subject (who/what) and the predicate (what's said about it).
panel.chemistry: The subject is the noun or pronoun doing or being something; the predicate contains the verb and everything that completes the thought.
panel.mixology: Every complete sentence needs both — a string of words with no subject-predicate pair is a fragment.
panel.examples: The tired dog | slept all afternoon. (subject | predicate)

panel.origin: A group of words containing a subject and a verb — the building block of sentences.
panel.chemistry: An independent clause stands alone as a sentence; a dependent (subordinate) clause cannot, because it begins with a word like 'because' or 'when'.
panel.mixology: A dependent clause left on its own is a fragment — 'Because it rained.' needs a main clause to complete it.
panel.examples: She smiled (independent) / because she won (dependent).

panel.origin: A group of related words that lacks a subject-verb pair, acting as a single unit.
panel.chemistry: Phrases (prepositional, noun, verb, participial) add information but can't stand alone as sentences because they have no subject doing a verb.
panel.mixology: Don't mistake a long phrase for a sentence — 'Running down the street' is a phrase, not a complete thought.
panel.examples: in the morning / the big red barn / running quickly (all phrases)

panel.origin: A simple sentence has one independent clause; a compound has two or more joined as equals.
panel.chemistry: Simple: one complete thought. Compound: two complete thoughts linked by a comma + conjunction, or a semicolon.
panel.mixology: Join two independent clauses with a comma AND a conjunction — a comma alone creates a comma splice.
panel.examples: I ran. (simple) / I ran, and she walked. (compound)

panel.origin: A sentence with one independent clause plus at least one dependent clause.
panel.chemistry: It pairs a complete thought with a subordinate one introduced by a word like because, although, when, or if.
panel.mixology: When the dependent clause comes first, put a comma after it; when it comes second, usually don't.
panel.examples: Although it rained, we went out. / We went out although it rained.

panel.origin: An incomplete sentence missing a subject, a verb, or a complete thought.
panel.chemistry: Fragments are pieces punctuated as if they were full sentences but can't stand alone grammatically.
panel.mixology: Fix a fragment by attaching it to a nearby sentence or adding the missing subject or verb.
panel.examples: Fragment: 'Running to the store.' Fix: 'She was running to the store.'

panel.origin: The rule that a verb must match its subject in number — singular subject, singular verb.
panel.chemistry: Singular subjects take singular verbs (The dog barks); plural subjects take plural verbs (The dogs bark).
panel.mixology: Watch for phrases between subject and verb — 'The box of chocolates IS', not 'are', because the subject is 'box'.
panel.examples: The list of items is long. The students are ready.

panel.origin: The rule that a pronoun must agree with the noun it replaces in number and gender.
panel.chemistry: A singular antecedent needs a singular pronoun; a plural one needs a plural pronoun. The pronoun should clearly point to one noun.
panel.mixology: Singular 'they' is now widely accepted for an unknown or nonbinary person ('Each student brought their book').
panel.examples: The players grabbed their gear. A nurse knows their patients.

panel.origin: Whether to use a subject pronoun (I, he, she, they) or object pronoun (me, him, her, them).
panel.chemistry: Subject pronouns do the action; object pronouns receive it. Test tricky pairs by dropping the other person.
panel.mixology: 'Between you and me', never 'between you and I' — after a preposition you need the object form.
panel.examples: She and I left. (subjects) / He gave it to her and me. (objects)

panel.origin: The principle that items in a series or comparison should share the same grammatical form.
panel.chemistry: List items, paired ideas, and comparisons read smoothly when they match: all nouns, all -ing verbs, all the same structure.
panel.mixology: Faulty: 'I like hiking, swimming, and to read.' Parallel: 'hiking, swimming, and reading.'
panel.examples: She is smart, kind, and generous. We came to see, to learn, and to grow.

panel.origin: The dot that ends a statement or command — the most basic full stop.
panel.chemistry: Use a period to close a declarative or imperative sentence, and in many abbreviations (Dr., etc.).
panel.mixology: Don't use a period to join two sentences into one — that needs a conjunction, semicolon, or staying separate.
panel.examples: The meeting is over. Please sit down.

panel.origin: The mark that ends a direct question.
panel.chemistry: Place it at the end of a direct question. Don't use it for indirect questions, which are really statements.
panel.mixology: Indirect questions take a period, not a question mark — 'I wonder what time it is.', not '...is?'
panel.examples: Direct: 'What time is it?' Indirect: 'She asked what time it was.'

panel.origin: The mark that signals strong emotion, emphasis, or a forceful command.
panel.chemistry: It ends an exclamatory sentence or a sharp command, conveying surprise, excitement, or urgency.
panel.mixology: Use it sparingly — overusing exclamation points drains their force and looks unprofessional.
panel.examples: Watch out! That's incredible! Stop right now!

panel.origin: The most versatile mark — it separates, groups, and clarifies within a sentence.
panel.chemistry: Commas separate list items, set off introductory and nonessential elements, and join clauses with a conjunction.
panel.mixology: A comma alone can't join two sentences (that's a comma splice) — add a conjunction or use a semicolon.
panel.examples: We bought apples, pears, and figs. After lunch, we left.

panel.origin: A mark that links two closely related complete sentences, or separates complex list items.
panel.chemistry: Use a semicolon to join two independent clauses without a conjunction, or to separate list items that already contain commas.
panel.mixology: Both sides of a semicolon must be able to stand alone as sentences — otherwise use a comma.
panel.examples: I came; I saw; I conquered. We visited Paris, France; Rome, Italy; and Bern.

panel.origin: A mark that introduces a list, an explanation, or a quotation — it points forward.
panel.chemistry: A colon follows a complete sentence and announces what comes next: a list, a definition, or an elaboration.
panel.mixology: Use a complete sentence before a colon — not 'The colors are: red, blue' but 'I bought three colors: red, blue, green.'
panel.examples: She had one goal: to win. Bring these: a pen, paper, and ID.

panel.origin: A versatile mark — the em dash — that adds emphasis, interruption, or an aside.
panel.chemistry: An em dash sets off a strong break, an abrupt change, or a parenthetical you want to spotlight rather than tuck away.
panel.mixology: Don't overuse dashes; too many make writing feel breathless. They're stronger than commas, looser than colons.
panel.examples: He grabbed the keys — and then froze. The plan — risky as it was — worked.

panel.origin: Curved marks that enclose extra, non-essential information or asides.
panel.chemistry: Parentheses tuck in a clarification, example, or aside that could be removed without breaking the sentence.
panel.mixology: Keep parenthetical asides brief; if the info is essential, it probably shouldn't be in parentheses.
panel.examples: The trip (our first abroad) was unforgettable. Call me (after 5 pm).

panel.origin: An error where two complete sentences are wrongly joined by only a comma.
panel.chemistry: A comma splice happens when a comma alone connects two independent clauses, which it can't do on its own.
panel.mixology: Fix it four ways: add a conjunction, swap in a semicolon, use a period, or subordinate one clause.
panel.examples: Wrong: 'It rained, we stayed home.' Right: 'It rained, so we stayed home.'

panel.origin: A mark that shows possession or marks the missing letters in a contraction.
panel.chemistry: Add 's for singular possession (dog's bone), just ' for plural possession (dogs' park), and use it for contractions (can't = cannot).
panel.mixology: Never use an apostrophe to make a plain plural — it's 'apples', not 'apple's'.
panel.examples: The cat's toy. The girls' team. It's late (it is). Don't go.

panel.origin: Marks that enclose direct speech, quoted text, or certain titles.
panel.chemistry: Use quotation marks around someone's exact words and around titles of short works. Periods and commas go inside them in US style.
panel.mixology: Don't use quotation marks for emphasis — that 'fresh' sign suggests the food is anything but.
panel.examples: She said, "Hello." The story is titled "The Gift."

panel.origin: A short mark that joins words or parts of words into a single unit.
panel.chemistry: Hyphens link compound modifiers before a noun (well-known author), join some prefixes, and split words at line ends.
panel.mixology: Don't confuse the short hyphen with the longer dash — the hyphen joins, the dash separates.
panel.examples: A well-known writer. Twenty-one. A state-of-the-art lab.

panel.origin: Three spaced dots that mark omitted words or a trailing-off thought.
panel.chemistry: An ellipsis (…) shows that text has been left out of a quotation, or signals a pause or unfinished thought.
panel.mixology: Don't scatter ellipses through casual writing for effect — they read as vague or hesitant.
panel.examples: She quoted, "We the people … form a union." I wonder…

panel.origin: Square marks that enclose editorial additions inside quoted material.
panel.chemistry: Square brackets [ ] mark words you insert into a quotation for clarity, distinct from the original speaker's words.
panel.mixology: Don't mix up square brackets with parentheses — brackets signal an editor's insertion, not the author's aside.
panel.examples: He said, "[The plan] worked perfectly." (clarifying who/what)

panel.origin: The classic mix-up: 'its' (belonging to it) versus 'it's' (it is / it has).
panel.chemistry: 'Its' is the possessive (the dog wagged its tail); 'it's' is always a contraction of 'it is' or 'it has'.
panel.mixology: Test it: if you can replace it with 'it is', use 'it's'. If not, use 'its'.
panel.examples: The cat licked its paws. It's raining. It's been a long day.

panel.origin: 'Your' (belonging to you) versus 'you're' (you are).
panel.chemistry: 'Your' shows possession; 'you're' is the contraction of 'you are'. They sound identical but mean different things.
panel.mixology: Test it: if 'you are' fits, write 'you're'. Otherwise it's 'your'.
panel.examples: Your book is here. You're going to love it.

panel.origin: The three sound-alikes: there (place), their (possession), they're (they are).
panel.chemistry: 'There' points to a place or fact; 'their' shows possession; 'they're' means 'they are'.
panel.mixology: Swap-test: 'they are' → they're; belongs to them → their; everything else → there.
panel.examples: They're putting their bags over there.

panel.origin: 'Affect' (usually a verb, to influence) versus 'effect' (usually a noun, a result).
panel.chemistry: Affect is normally the action ('the rain affected traffic'); effect is normally the result ('the effect was chaos').
panel.mixology: Trick: A=Action (affect), E=End result (effect). The rare exceptions ('effect change') confuse even pros.
panel.examples: The news affected her. The effect was immediate.

panel.origin: 'Who' (subject) versus 'whom' (object) — the formal distinction many people skip.
panel.chemistry: Use 'who' when it's doing the action, 'whom' when it's receiving it. 'Whom' follows prepositions in formal use.
panel.mixology: Test: answer with he/him — 'he' → who, 'him' → whom. ('To whom?' → 'to him.')
panel.examples: Who called? (he called) / To whom did you give it? (to him)

panel.origin: Two or more complete sentences crammed together without proper punctuation.
panel.chemistry: A run-on (or fused sentence) joins independent clauses with nothing between them, leaving the reader breathless.
panel.mixology: Fix by splitting into separate sentences, or joining with a semicolon or a comma + conjunction.
panel.examples: Wrong: 'I ran I was late.' Right: 'I ran because I was late.'

panel.origin: A descriptive phrase that accidentally attaches to the wrong subject — often unintentionally funny.
panel.chemistry: A modifier 'dangles' when the thing it's supposed to describe isn't actually in the sentence, so it lands on the wrong noun.
panel.mixology: Make sure the word right after an opening modifier is the thing it describes.
panel.examples: Wrong: 'Walking home, the rain started.' (the rain wasn't walking) Right: 'Walking home, I felt the rain start.'

PARTS OF SPEECH.en: English has eight traditional parts of speech (some count nine with articles), and a single word can shift among them — 'book' is a noun in 'read a book' but a verb in 'book a flight'.
PARTS OF SPEECH.es: English has eight traditional parts of speech (some count nine with articles), and a single word can shift among them — 'book' is a noun in 'read a book' but a verb in 'book a flight'.
PARTS OF SPEECH.fr: English has eight traditional parts of speech (some count nine with articles), and a single word can shift among them — 'book' is a noun in 'read a book' but a verb in 'book a flight'.
PARTS OF SPEECH.it: English has eight traditional parts of speech (some count nine with articles), and a single word can shift among them — 'book' is a noun in 'read a book' but a verb in 'book a flight'.

VERBS & TENSE.en: English technically has only two true tenses — present and past. The 'future' isn't a verb ending at all; we build it with helper words like 'will' and 'going to'.
VERBS & TENSE.es: English technically has only two true tenses — present and past. The 'future' isn't a verb ending at all; we build it with helper words like 'will' and 'going to'.
VERBS & TENSE.fr: English technically has only two true tenses — present and past. The 'future' isn't a verb ending at all; we build it with helper words like 'will' and 'going to'.
VERBS & TENSE.it: English technically has only two true tenses — present and past. The 'future' isn't a verb ending at all; we build it with helper words like 'will' and 'going to'.

SENTENCE STRUCTURE.en: The shortest grammatically complete sentence in English is just two words — 'I am' — or even one in the imperative: 'Go.' (the subject 'you' is understood).
SENTENCE STRUCTURE.es: The shortest grammatically complete sentence in English is just two words — 'I am' — or even one in the imperative: 'Go.' (the subject 'you' is understood).
SENTENCE STRUCTURE.fr: The shortest grammatically complete sentence in English is just two words — 'I am' — or even one in the imperative: 'Go.' (the subject 'you' is understood).
SENTENCE STRUCTURE.it: The shortest grammatically complete sentence in English is just two words — 'I am' — or even one in the imperative: 'Go.' (the subject 'you' is understood).

AGREEMENT & CASE.en: Singular 'they' isn't new — writers from Shakespeare to Jane Austen used it for an unknown person centuries before the modern debate.
AGREEMENT & CASE.es: Singular 'they' isn't new — writers from Shakespeare to Jane Austen used it for an unknown person centuries before the modern debate.
AGREEMENT & CASE.fr: Singular 'they' isn't new — writers from Shakespeare to Jane Austen used it for an unknown person centuries before the modern debate.
AGREEMENT & CASE.it: Singular 'they' isn't new — writers from Shakespeare to Jane Austen used it for an unknown person centuries before the modern debate.

END PUNCTUATION.en: The question mark and exclamation point may both descend from Latin: 'quaestio' (question) and 'io' (joy), abbreviated and stacked into the marks we use today.
END PUNCTUATION.es: The question mark and exclamation point may both descend from Latin: 'quaestio' (question) and 'io' (joy), abbreviated and stacked into the marks we use today.
END PUNCTUATION.fr: The question mark and exclamation point may both descend from Latin: 'quaestio' (question) and 'io' (joy), abbreviated and stacked into the marks we use today.
END PUNCTUATION.it: The question mark and exclamation point may both descend from Latin: 'quaestio' (question) and 'io' (joy), abbreviated and stacked into the marks we use today.

INTERNAL PUNCTUATION.en: The serial (Oxford) comma — the one before 'and' in a list — has launched lawsuits; one 2017 case over a missing comma cost a dairy company about $5 million.
INTERNAL PUNCTUATION.es: The serial (Oxford) comma — the one before 'and' in a list — has launched lawsuits; one 2017 case over a missing comma cost a dairy company about $5 million.
INTERNAL PUNCTUATION.fr: The serial (Oxford) comma — the one before 'and' in a list — has launched lawsuits; one 2017 case over a missing comma cost a dairy company about $5 million.
INTERNAL PUNCTUATION.it: The serial (Oxford) comma — the one before 'and' in a list — has launched lawsuits; one 2017 case over a missing comma cost a dairy company about $5 million.

OTHER MARKS.en: The apostrophe is the most-abused mark in English — the misplaced 'greengrocer's apostrophe' (Apple's for sale) is common enough to have its own name.
OTHER MARKS.es: The apostrophe is the most-abused mark in English — the misplaced 'greengrocer's apostrophe' (Apple's for sale) is common enough to have its own name.
OTHER MARKS.fr: The apostrophe is the most-abused mark in English — the misplaced 'greengrocer's apostrophe' (Apple's for sale) is common enough to have its own name.
OTHER MARKS.it: The apostrophe is the most-abused mark in English — the misplaced 'greengrocer's apostrophe' (Apple's for sale) is common enough to have its own name.

COMMON PITFALLS.en: 'Its' vs 'it's' trips up native speakers constantly because the rule feels backwards — for once, the possessive form is the one WITHOUT the apostrophe.
COMMON PITFALLS.es: 'Its' vs 'it's' trips up native speakers constantly because the rule feels backwards — for once, the possessive form is the one WITHOUT the apostrophe.
COMMON PITFALLS.fr: 'Its' vs 'it's' trips up native speakers constantly because the rule feels backwards — for once, the possessive form is the one WITHOUT the apostrophe.
COMMON PITFALLS.it: 'Its' vs 'it's' trips up native speakers constantly because the rule feels backwards — for once, the possessive form is the one WITHOUT the apostrophe.
